headline: CRUDE RUNS 6%, CORN SPLIT WIDENS, BEANS HOLD
subheadline: Trump's Iran blockade reinstatement hit oil overnight; corn carries the pollination discount forward while soybeans push back toward $12.10 November resistance.
lead: Crude oil had its biggest single-session gain in months, running nearly 6% to $78.66 after President Trump reinstated the Iran blockade Monday, resetting the energy premium that had been quietly deflating for weeks. The corn market isn't listening: nearby held at $4.41 while December fell another 4.5 cents to $4.63, the old-crop/new-crop split now 22 cents wide and widening. The 2026 planting season, behind the average through May before catching up between rain systems, is now squarely inside pollination, and northeast Missouri farmers are praying for rain. The weekly thread question is alive: does Monday's crop progress confirm the good-crop thesis, or does field-level stress put the weather premium back on the board? Right now, beans are the market with an answer.
the_takeaway: Crude is loud today; corn's quiet split is the story that matters to your operation.
teaser: Oil runs 6% on Iran blockade; corn split hits 22 cents; beans push toward resistance.

one_number.unit: USDA beef export data revision magnitude
one_number.context: USDA dramatically lowered its reported beef export sales by 90% on Thursday, a revision that sparks real questions about data quality at the agency. For cattle producers pricing against export demand, that is not a rounding error. It changes the fundamental supply picture and raises the question of how much export-demand optimism was already baked into live cattle prices sitting at $234.95.

yesterdays_call.summary: Yesterday's call was beans up, targeting a close above $12.10 November.
yesterdays_call.note: November beans closed at $11.935, short of the $12.10 target. The biofuel-driven bid was real -- nearby ran to $12.07 -- but new-crop never got there. Miss. The $12.10 level is still the line; Thursday export sales are the next shot at it.

sections.title: Crude Reruns the Premium
sections.body: WTI crude ran to **$78.66**, a nearly 6% gain in a single session, after Trump announced the U.S. would reinstate its blockade on Iran, forcing traders to reprice Hormuz disruption risk that had been slowly unwinding for weeks. Iran-Hormuz tensions, with the Strait of Hormuz premium that built since early April now deflating on diplomatic progress, just got a second chapter: Monday's policy move re-exposed everything the market had given back. Oil refiners are already cashing in, with diesel and gasoline staying expensive even as crude had retreated, and that refiner margin story now gets a new tailwind. For producers booking fall harvest diesel, the window that looked attractive last week is closing. Natural gas held flat at $2.90, the quietest contract in the room today.
sections.catalyst: Trump reinstatement of Iran blockade announced Monday, crude ran more than 8% per policy sources before settling near 6%.
sections.bottom_line: Hormuz re-escalation erases weeks of premium deflation; lock fall diesel before the spread widens further.
sections.farmer_action: Producers who didn't lock harvest diesel last week: the $78.66 WTI print changes that calculus. Basis-adjusted diesel prices will move with crude over the next 48 hours. If you have storage, this week is better than next.
sections.vs_yesterday: Crude: reversed the entire deflation narrative in one session.

sections.title: Corn Split Holds, Pollination Clock Ticks
sections.body: Nearby corn eased just a fraction to **$4.41** while December fell 4.5 cents to **$4.63**, a 22-cent spread that is still telling you the market sees new-crop risk and old-crop irrelevance at the same time. On the ground in northeast Missouri, farmers are crossing their fingers through dry pollination, with one grower saying it point-blank to Brownfield: 'We have our fingers crossed for a rain.' That field-level stress is exactly what the old-crop/new-crop spread has been pricing. South Dakota's winter wheat harvest is running better than expected, which keeps the broader small-grain complex from generating sympathy weather bids in corn. Monday's crop progress numbers at 3 PM CT set the next chapter; a good/excellent condition print above 65% confirms new-crop is fine and December holds near current levels. Below 60% reopens the weather premium conversation toward $4.75.
sections.catalyst: Dry pollination stress reported in northeast Missouri; South Dakota wheat running better than expected, removing small-grain sympathy bid.
sections.bottom_line: The 22-cent split is still the only honest weather premium calculator in this market.
sections.vs_yesterday: Corn: split widened a cent; field reports confirm the stress the spread was already pricing.

sections.title: Soybeans Push, Meal Fades
sections.body: Soybeans nearby ran to **$12.07**, up more than 10 cents, while November held at **$11.94** and pulled back a fraction. The divergence inside the complex is the real tell: soybean oil ran **2.1%** to **$71.09** while meal dropped **1.2%** to **$315.60**. That is not a demand story, it is a crush composition story. The biofuel and vegetable oil side of crush is bidding for beans; the feed side is not. Missouri farmers say soybeans look as good as ever despite the dry stretch hitting corn, which is a bearish data point for November if field reports are correct. The $12.10 November level is the line for new-crop bulls; today's close at $11.94 says the market didn't get there but wants another shot. Thursday's export sales are the catalyst that either provides the excuse to push through or confirms the consolidation.
sections.catalyst: Soybean oil running 2.1% on biofuel demand; meal weakness signals feed-side softness; Missouri field reports show strong bean conditions vs. stressed corn.
sections.bottom_line: Oil leading, meal lagging: this is a biofuel bid, not a broad demand story.
sections.farmer_action: Unpriced new-crop soybeans: hold into Thursday export sales. Above 400K MT exports, $12.10 November is the scale-sale window. Below 300K MT, the bid fades and storage economics dominate.
sections.vs_yesterday: Beans: oil/meal divergence now the dominant intra-complex story.

sections.title: Cattle Quiet, Beef Data Problem Isn't
sections.body: Live cattle barely moved, settling at **$234.95**, down a tenth of a percent. Feeders matched the quiet at **$354.20**. But the 90% USDA beef export data revision is not a quiet story. USDA dramatically lowered reported beef export sales, a move that Reuters flagged as raising fresh concerns about data quality. For live cattle producers sitting at a 52-week range position of 56% from the low, pricing decisions made against that export demand signal may need revisiting. The ongoing Cargill Fort Morgan/Schuyler plant lockout that began May 19 is still creating processing-constrained dynamics, and today's USDA Unified Agenda lists the rescission of three Packers and Stockyards Act proposed rules, which won't help producers seeking transparency in packer pricing. Lean hogs drifted to **$98.15**, off less than a percent, well above yesterday's call level of $90.
sections.catalyst: USDA 90% beef export data revision published Thursday; USDA Unified Agenda targets Packers and Stockyards Act rule rescissions.
sections.bottom_line: Cattle price discovery just got harder: the data you were pricing against may have been wrong.
sections.vs_yesterday: Cattle: beef export data revision changes the supply picture; hogs held well above $90 call.

outside_the_pit.title: FTC Settlement Gives Farmers Right to Repair John Deere
outside_the_pit.body: After decades of forcing producers through authorized dealers only, a federal settlement now secures independent repair rights for John Deere equipment. For operators running large equipment lineups, this matters at the margin of harvest timing: a breakdown during pollination or grain-fill that used to mean a 48-hour dealer wait can now mean a local shop or your own tools.

outside_the_pit.title: Oklahoma Poultry Pollution Settlement Hits Integrators for $41.7M
outside_the_pit.body: Integrators in Oklahoma must pay $41.7 million into a state environmental fund and help contract growers install riparian buffers following a poultry pollution suit settlement. For contract poultry growers, the buffer requirement is a new input cost coming in an era when contract margins are already thin.

outside_the_pit.title: OPEC Cuts Demand Forecast for Third Straight Month
outside_the_pit.body: Even as crude ran nearly 6% today on the Iran blockade news, OPEC trimmed its 2026 global oil demand growth forecast for the third consecutive month. That structural demand softness is the counterweight to the geopolitical premium: the market is pricing a supply shock, not a demand boom, which means the Hormuz run has a ceiling if diplomatic channels reopen.

spread_to_watch.label: Soybean Oil / Soybean Meal ratio
spread_to_watch.level: Oil at $71.09 up 2.1%, meal at $315.60 down 1.2%; oil share of crush value widening
spread_to_watch.commentary: When oil runs and meal fades simultaneously, the crush complex is pricing a biofuel call, not a feed demand call. This spread is telling you that renewable diesel and biodiesel demand is doing more work for beans right now than global protein demand. Producers pricing new-crop soybeans should watch whether this ratio holds or reverts: if meal firms back toward $320, the bean bid gets broader and more durable.

basis.headline: Corn basis soft Belt-wide; wheat basis firming at elevator.
basis.body: Eastern Belt corn basis is holding soft as old-crop movement slows ahead of pollination uncertainty. No urgency from merchandisers to pull bushels forward. **Wheat basis is firming at the elevator level** across the northern Plains as South Dakota harvest runs better-than-expected and millers compete for early-delivery bushels. Soybean basis is quiet, consistent with the seasonal pattern ahead of August delivery decisions.

weekly_thread.question: Will USDA Crop Progress confirm the good-crop thesis that nearby corn is already pricing, or does a condition decline put the weather premium back on the board?
weekly_thread.status_text: Field reports from northeast Missouri are the first real-world data point this week: dry conditions during corn pollination are the exact stress the December discount has been pricing. Monday's Crop Progress at 3 PM CT is the next resolution event; below 60% good/excellent and the December discount looks cheap.

the_more_you_know.title: The soybean crush: when oil and meal stop moving together, the market is telling you something.
the_more_you_know.body: Today soybean oil ran 2.1% to $71.09 while soybean meal dropped 1.2% to $315.60. Normally they move roughly together because both come out of the same bean. When they split hard, it means one end of the crush equation has a demand driver the other doesn't. Right now, renewable diesel mandates and biodiesel blending demand are bidding aggressively for soy oil, while global protein feed demand has cooled. The crush miller captures that spread: buy the bean, sell both products, pocket the margin. What it means for producers is that bean demand is real but narrow. If the biofuel policy tailwind under current EPA blending standards softens, so does this bid. The 52-week high positioning of nearby beans at 94% from the low is largely a biofuel story. Keep that in mind when pricing new crop.

watch_list.desc: USDA Crop Progress: corn good/excellent below 60% reopens $4.75 December; above 65% confirms new-crop discount holds.
Weekly Export Sales: soybeans above 400K MT gives bulls the excuse to push November through $12.10; below 300K MT, the biofuel bid is carrying the whole load alone.
Iran blockade follow-through: does diplomatic back-channel via Swiss intermediaries reopen? Any progress report caps the crude run and starts the Hormuz premium deflating again.

Cargill Fort Morgan/Schuyler lockout: no resolution announced. Each additional week removes roughly 2% of weekly US slaughter capacity. Watch for live cattle to feel the processing constraint more acutely as fed cattle numbers back up.
USDA beef export data revision fallout: any agency clarification or methodology explanation changes the cattle pricing picture. Watch for Reuters follow-up.

daily_quote.text: A bad penny always turns up.

source_summary: CME Group settlement prices; USDA Crop Progress; Brownfield Ag News field reports (Missouri, South Dakota, Michigan); Reuters/USDA beef export data revision; OilPrice.com Iran blockade and OPEC demand forecast; Farm Policy News; The Fence Post; UFCW; FTC settlement reporting.
